column3: The foundations of civilization are eroding...again Our current way of doing things, our institutions, that guide our norms and values have lost their way. We are failing because of something even more foundational: The transmission of ideas and our tool for doing it, language. We need to rediscover ourselves...again... Much of what I have to say isn't new - some of it, I think, will be - but much of it might be new to you. I'm striving to rebuild Foundational Understanding If we ask why enough times we get to Fundamental Questions, like: • Why is there something rather than nothing?[1] • What is reality and how does it work?[2] • Why are we here?[3] Unfortunately, we don't have answers to these questions. We are ignorant, but awareness of that puts us in pretty good company... That awareness lets us start figuring out how to be less ignorant. That we have the capacity to bring the confidence in our belief close to what is true. We have the capacity to Reason.
column4:
The Foundations of our civilization are eroding. Again.

This has happened before, but it does not have to happen forever.

We've known what causes it, and how to fix it, for nearly all of recorded history.
column5:
The foundations of our civilization are eroding. Again.

This has happened before, but it does not have to happen forever. 

We can fix it, and we've known how for...well...nearly all of history. 

You're probably familiar with the game telephone, where you say a word or phrase and pass it around in a circle till it gets back to the originator and they see how much it has been mutated or corrupted. Now imagine trying to maintain everything that is important across generations. How would one do that? 

We are eroding because you, dear reader, most likely have not heard of Plato's Paideia (παιδεία), which he tried to bring to life at his Academy.  Note: The etymology of the world is as much an accident of history as how we understand Politics in Left vs Right. 

You might know some other terms for similar ideas (the academy/academia, education, the liberal arts, the humanities, or simply memes - the last of which is almost a perfect example of what I'll discuss next). These are the we have terms we use for how we attempt to do our best at the transmission of ideas and our tool for doing it, language.

Trying to maintain continuity across generation is hard, Paideia was the idea of how to achieve it. However, terms are imperfect means of transmitting concepts. They are vulnerable to misunderstanding, mishearing, misinterpreting, and active corruption. 

Dawkins meme wasn't exactly novel, but it was a another good way to describe how ideas transmit, and like genes it is a recognition of that imperfection of transmission. That the term itself go so quickly mutated is a unfortunate and almost perfect irony. 

The reason you may be unfamiliar with the term Paedeia is due to our institutions failing. The Academy died in the Universities, not because they lack power but because they've lost their purpose, because they've betrayed you and society, again. 

And because this has happened before we have another ancient term for what they have become, σοφιστής (sophistēs) or sophists. Deceivers who pretend to sell wisdom and mostly sell something else. Our Universities today are overrun with them, and the same concern that Socrates had is there today, claiming you you are wise and locking that wisdom behind pay walls. JSTOR, Journals Aaron Schwartz, the massive cost of tuition and its rise relative to inflation, especially in digital age - a time of post-scarcity knowledge. The universities are the contemporary sophists. They are the enemy Socrates fought and Plato warned us of. 

And the sophists have enabled our erosion our decay, our rot. Not only have they killed important terms like the Academy they have let our language around power become incoherent, they have let them and to some degree encouraged them to become hollowed out and used as tribal markers. 

Our discourse has collapsed into emotional activation. And the people who should be diagnosing this, the  supposedly educated, the credentialed, the "expert class" are instead sophsits the cause part of the problem, the rot, trading on the symbols of wisdom without practicing it.

I'm striving to be a small voince in correcting this, and it starts as do most things, with building a Foundation. In this cause a Foundation of Understanding.
column6:
Examples of the pattern — expandable list]
This pattern recurs across civilizations, cultures, and millennia:
- Athens built Plato's Academy and lost it to doctrine.
- The Islamic Golden Age built the House of Wisdom and lost it when revelation was declared superior to reason.
- China built a 1,300-year examination system and watched it decay into rote memorization.
- Rome's philosopher-king couldn't transmit wisdom to his own son.
- Nalanda concentrated a millennium of Buddhist scholarship in one institution and lost it all when that institution burned.
- Japan chose maximum conservation through 250 years of isolation and found itself defenseless when reality changed.
Each of these is explored in detail in  [links to supporting articles/resources]

Other people who propose cycles:
Ibn Khaldun (14th century) is arguably the OG. The Arab historian argued that civilizations rise and fall in roughly 3-4 generation cycles (~120 years). His concept of asabiyyah — group cohesion or social solidarity — is the key variable. A tough, hungry group with strong bonds conquers or builds a civilization. Success brings luxury and comfort, which erodes that cohesion over generations. The grandchildren of empire-builders become soft, entitled, and fragmented. A new group with stronger asabiyyah then displaces them. Dalio's framework borrows heavily from this logic whether he acknowledges it or not.
Oswald Spengler (The Decline of the West, 1918) argued civilizations are like organisms — they're born, mature, and die following an internal biological clock. He identified a ~1,000-year lifespan for major civilizations and claimed Western civilization had entered its terminal "winter" phase. Unlike Dalio, Spengler was a hard determinist — he thought decline was essentially inevitable and not fixable by policy.
Arnold Toynbee (A Study of History, 1934-61) studied 21 civilizations and argued they rise through a "creative minority" that successfully responds to challenges. They decline when that elite becomes a "dominant minority" — still in power but no longer creative or legitimate, ruling by force rather than inspiration. The masses withdraw their loyalty, and internal "proletariats" and external "barbarians" pull the civilization apart. More optimistic than Spengler — he thought civilizations could renew themselves through spiritual or moral transformation.
Peter Turchin (Ages of Discord, 2016) is probably the most rigorous modern version. His "cliodynamics" framework uses quantitative data to identify ~50-year cycles driven by elite overproduction (too many people competing for too few elite positions), declining real wages for workers, and state fiscal crisis. He predicted in 2010 that the US would hit a peak of political instability around 2020 — which turned out to be uncomfortably accurate. His mechanism is more specific than Dalio's: when you produce more elites than the system can absorb, the frustrated would-be elites radicalize and mobilize the discontented masses.
Strauss & Howe (The Fourth Turning, 1997) proposed an ~80-year generational cycle with four "turnings" — High, Awakening, Unraveling, Crisis. They predicted a major crisis period starting around 2005-2025. Their framework is more cultural/generational than economic, arguing that the character of each generation (shaped by which turning they grew up in) drives the next turning.
Sir John Glubb (The Fate of Empires, 1978) — a British military officer who studied 11 empires and found a remarkably consistent ~250-year lifespan, progressing through ages of Pioneers, Commerce, Affluence, Intellect, and Decadence. His markers of terminal decline: an obsession with celebrity culture, influx of foreigners, welfare state expansion, and a shift from duty to selfishness. Less rigorous than the others but widely cited.

Ray Dalio: 6 stage model
column7:
The thing that was supposed that guide our norms and values have lost their way. That thing is the Academy something that our Universities killed.

If you ask why enough times, all of this traces back to the same place.



Athens had it. The Renaissance recovered it. The Enlightenment built on it. Each time, the core insight was the same. And each time, it faded — not because it was wrong, but because precision is expensive and stories are cheap. Maintaining a deep understanding of why your civilization works requires constant effort. Letting it slide into sloganeering requires nothing at all.
column9:
Examples of the pattern — see existing Notes column for the full civilizational cycles list (Ibn Khaldun, Spengler, Toynbee, Turchin, Strauss & Howe, Glubb, Dalio).

Column 4 contains an alternate draft of the telephone metaphor that was developed into Row 4's final version. Column 6 contains the "Athens had it, Renaissance recovered it, Enlightenment built on it" passage — this language was cut from the rework but the cadence is strong; consider recovering for another article.

column4:
You're probably familiar with the game telephone: a phrase is whispered person to person around a circle and often arrives back mutated beyond recognition.

Now imagine the phrase is everything your civilization figured out about how to think, what to believe, and how to live well. Now imagine the circle is measured in generations, and the original phrase is itself imperfect and in need of an update. Each generation must pass the phrase on without losing it, make it less wrong, and persuade the next generation that the change is an improvement rather than a corruption.

That is the core problem. 

The transmission of understanding across generations is extraordinarily difficult, and the collective improvement of that understanding harder still.
column9:
The telephone metaphor is imperfect in one important way: in the game, mutations are random. In civilizational transmission, mutations can be random (honest misunderstanding), motivated (power-seeking, simplification for convenience), or structural (each generation's shortcuts become the next generation's starting point).

Michael Tomasello, The Cultural Origins of Human Cognition (1999): cumulative culture requires each generation to faithfully transmit AND improve. Failure at either step breaks "the ratchet." This is the formal version of the telephone problem.

"The original phrase is itself imperfect and in need of an update" — this is what makes the problem harder than mere preservation. Pure conservation would be difficult enough. But the phrase also needs to get better. Which means you need people capable of both receiving it accurately AND improving it — which requires the very wisdom the transmission is trying to produce. A bootstrapping problem.

column4: Historically we have built grand temples, some taking hundreds of years to construct while lasting thousands, as symbols for how important these ideas are. Elaborate stories and rituals often accompany them as methods for passing that understanding on.
column6: Give list of old temples and build times from aorund the world.
column9:
Grand temples and their construction timescales:
- Great Pyramid of Giza (c. 2560 BCE) — ~20 years to build, still standing after 4,500 years
- Parthenon, Athens (447–432 BCE) — 15 years
- Angkor Wat, Cambodia (early 12th century) — ~30 years
- Chartres Cathedral, France (1194–1220) — 26 years
- Notre-Dame de Paris (1163–1345) — 182 years
- Cologne Cathedral (1248–1880) — 632 years
- Sagrada Família, Barcelona (1882–ongoing) — 140+ years and counting
- Borobudur, Java (c. 750–842 CE) — ~75 years

These are investment signals. Civilizations don't spend generations building structures unless they consider the ideas those structures represent to be foundational. The temples themselves are transmission devices — their permanence is the message: "this matters enough to outlast us."

The stories and rituals that accompany them serve the same function. The Eleusinian Mysteries lasted ~2,000 years. The Catholic Mass has been performed continuously for ~1,700 years. Confucian rites persist after 2,500 years. The durability of ritual often exceeds the durability of understanding — which is the problem.

column4:
Yet, understanding still erodes, forgotten, mutated, or corrupted. Rituals and beliefs survive while the purposes behind them are forgotten. Symbols are defended long after the understanding that gave them meaning has been lost or mutated. Often the very institutions built to preserve understanding become vessels for its corruption.

This pattern is old, ancient even, so are both the diagnosis and the remedy.
column5:
Paideia is an unfamiliar term to most, an example of the transmission losing the signal.

A significant reason for this loss of signal is that the institution founded to maintain it, the Academy, has failed, and not for the first time.

Not because they lack resources or power, but because the people running it are repeating an all too familiar pattern of failure.

The ancient Greeks saw this failure mode in the Sophists, σοφισταί: teachers who sold lessons in wisdom, yet became a historic example of the gap between claiming wisdom and possessing it.
People who performed the appearance of wisdom without practicing it. Who sold credentials instead of cultivating understanding. Socrates spent his life fighting them. Plato built the Academy specifically as an alternative to them.

The pattern has returned. Our universities, the institutions that inherited the Academy's name and mission, have become the contemporary sophists. They trade on the symbols of wisdom while the substance decays.
column9:
"Symbols defended long after understanding lost" — this is the master pattern:
- Cargo cults: after WWII, Pacific Islanders built imitation airstrips hoping planes would return. Perfect preservation of form, zero access to function. Feynman used this as his metaphor for bad science ("Cargo Cult Science," 1974 Caltech commencement).
- Chesterton's Fence (The Thing, 1929): "Don't ever take a fence down until you know the reason it was put up." The conservative principle. But also: sometimes the reason is genuinely forgotten, and defending the fence becomes the obstacle.
- "Often the very institutions built to preserve understanding become vessels for its corruption" — the Catholic Church preserving Greek texts while suppressing their application. Universities preserving the word "Academy" while gutting its function. Political parties preserving the word "liberal" while practicing illiberalism.

Column 4 contains a draft connecting this directly to Paideia and the Sophists — material that was developed into Rows 7-8 in the final version.

column3: Reason is humanity's, so far unique, capacity to find truth. Utilizing it we developed methods to learn about Reality. Allowing us to start justifying our beliefs as knowledge/Scientia/science. There is much work left to be done in expanding these categories of knowledge or 'the Sciences' but we've made great strides toward understanding Reality, and in doing so improved our ability to Reason, as we can take notice if when our current tools for finding truth don't quite get us to reality so we then work to improve them. Our best methods of acquiring knowledge we refer to as our scientific methods, and we've added various tools to these methods over time and will keep finding more to add. This allowed us to start down the path of answering one of our fundamental questions. What is existence and how does it work?[2] Acquiring this knowledge hasn't been easy, its taken us thousands of years to get here. Partly because society erodes, and finding the Right Way to produce a Good Life or Just City hasn't been easy either.
column4: An Ancient Failure Mode The Classical Greeks encountered a version of the same failure we are experiencing now. Socrates became enduringly important because he exposed this decay and his legacy reversed it. In his time Sophists, σοφιστής, claimed authority over cultivating the minds of the next generation. Performing the role of wise teacher without practicing wisdom themselves, and charging a tidy sum to do it. Socrates fought the Sophists for the mind of Athens, won, and died for it. His victory resonates through all that came after. Socrates' student, Plato, taught at a grove near Athens. That grove was named after Akademos, an older Greek hero whose name survives largely because Plato happened to teach there. From that accident of history we get the term “the Academy". Akademos, the Academy, Academia became an institution. Its role was to follow in the footsteps of Socrates and those that came after in this millennia-long game of telephone. Yet the same thing that happened with the Sophists is happening with the Academy. People claiming the mantle of wisdom and charging a ridiculous rate for something they aren't actually providing. Plato's student Aristotle spoke of hoi eleutheroi (οἱ ἐλεύθεροι), the skills and knowledge a person needs to be worthy of freedom, of self-governance. Latin turned this into the Liberal Arts and eventually the Humanities. The deeper meaning behind the Academy, the Liberal Arts and the Humanities is Paideia (παιδεία).
column5:
A significant reason for this loss of signal is that the institution founded to maintain it, the Academy, has failed, and not for the first time. This isn't new. The pattern is old enough that it should be familiar: a society builds something extraordinary on a set of ideas, those ideas get transmitted imperfectly across generations, the transmission degrades, and eventually people are defending symbols whose meaning they've forgotten. The rituals survive. The understanding doesn't.

Not because they lack resources or power, but because the people running it are repeating an all too familiar pattern of failure.

The ancient Greeks had a term for this failure mode, too. They called them σοφιστής, sophists.
People who performed the appearance of wisdom without practicing it. Who sold credentials instead of cultivating understanding. Socrates spent his life fighting them. Plato built the Academy specifically as an alternative to them.

The pattern has returned. Our universities, the institutions that inherited the Academy's name and mission, have become the contemporary sophists. They trade on the symbols of wisdom while the substance decays.

Asides about another accident of history being left and right?
column6:
The evidence is structural, not anecdotal: - Journals locked behind paywalls in a post-scarcity digital age. Aaron Swartz saw this clearly and paid for it. - Tuition costs rising at multiples of inflation for decades while the core Academy function — teaching people to think — has been gutted in favor of vocational training the university can't do well. - Textbook prices that are indefensible by any honest economic analysis. - The field that studies scarcity producing institutions that manufacture artificial scarcity. This is explored fully in [Education article link]

Include link to signaling theory of education
column9:
Sophists: Protagoras, Gorgias, Hippias, Prodicus were the major figures. Not uniformly bad — Protagoras's "man is the measure of all things" is a serious philosophical position. But the commercial model selected for persuasion skill over truth-seeking. The corruption wasn't in charging fees per se but in what the fees optimized for.

Socrates's trial: 399 BCE. Charges: impiety (asebeia) and corrupting the youth. Convicted by jury of 501 Athenian citizens. Vote was close — roughly 280 to 221. Refused to flee (see Crito). Drank hemlock. See Plato's Apology, Crito, Phaedo for the trial, the argument against escape, and the death scene respectively.

Akademos/Hekademos: mythological Attic hero. The grove (Akademia) was about a mile northwest of Athens. Plato began teaching there c. 387 BCE. The Academy continued in various forms for nearly 900 years until Justinian I closed it in 529 CE — itself an act of a Christian emperor suppressing pagan philosophy. The referent killed by institutional power.

Hoi eleutheroi (οἱ ἐλεύθεροι): Aristotle, Politics. The "free arts" — skills befitting a free person, as opposed to banausic (mechanical/servile) arts. The distinction was explicitly political: you need these capacities to participate in self-governance. A person without them is free in name only.

"Signaling theory of education" — Bryan Caplan, The Case Against Education (2018): argues that much of modern education's value is signaling (proving you can sit through 4 years of compliance) rather than human capital formation (actually learning useful things). This is the quantitative version of the sophistry critique.

See existing Notes column for structural evidence: journals behind paywalls, Aaron Swartz, tuition inflation, textbook prices, artificial scarcity.

Column 2 (Original) contains an older version that led with Reason rather than the Socrates story — the structural pivot to leading with the historical narrative was a significant improvement.

column3:
Plato tried to built the first version of The Academy. Aristotle The Liberal Arts were an attempt to institutionalize it, the skills "worthy of a free person," the capacities needed for self-governance. The entire tradition of what we now call the humanities was, at its root, an answer to the telephone problem: how do you transmit the signal without degrading it?

Plato tried to build it at the first Academy. The liberal arts were an attempt to institutionalize it, the skills "worthy of a free person," the capacities needed for self-governance. The entire tradition of what we now call the humanities was, at its root, an answer to the telephone problem: how do you transmit the signal without degrading it?
column4: Paideia The deliberate cultivation and transmission of everything a civilization needs its citizens to understand, know, believe, and be capable of. Not just facts. Not just skills. The full formation of a person capable of participating in, sustaining, and improving a free and just society. The transmission of understanding. It is what 'being educated' should mean, as this was always the higher purpose of education. When that purpose is forgotten, education decays into something narrower: information without judgment, skill without orientation, credentials without substance. When that happens, a civilization may continue to educate in some technical sense while failing to form the kinds of people it depends on. That is where we are now. Most people no longer know the term, and fewer understand it. That, too, is part of the problem.
column5:
A significant reason for this loss of signal is that the institution founded to maintain it, the Academy, has failed, and not for the first time. This isn't new. The pattern is old enough that it should be familiar: a society builds something extraordinary on a set of ideas, those ideas get transmitted imperfectly across generations, the transmission degrades, and eventually people are defending symbols whose meaning they've forgotten. The rituals survive. The understanding doesn't.

Not because they lack resources or power, but because the people running it are repeating an all too familiar pattern of failure.

The ancient Greeks had a term for this failure mode, too. They called them σοφιστής, sophists.
People who performed the appearance of wisdom without practicing it. Who sold credentials instead of cultivating understanding. Socrates spent his life fighting them. Plato built the Academy specifically as an alternative to them.

The pattern has returned. Our universities, the institutions that inherited the Academy's name and mission, have become the contemporary sophists. They trade on the symbols of wisdom while the substance decays.
column6:
Academy:
Every "academy" in the world is named after the original Akademia of Athens — which was itself named after a sacred grove dedicated to the mythic hero Academus, or possibly derived from a word meaning "far away suburb." The connection between the word and its meaning is as much an accident of history as how we understand politics through Left vs Right — a topic addressed in [link].

Paideia:
Paideia is a term largely lost but it meaning lives in the term Academy, or it used to, and we are now losing the meaning of that and other related terms. Education, Liberal Arts, Humanities. Each a conceptual predecessor of Paideia, and today each eroded, mutated, corrupted to mean little of their substative concepts. 

 It is the Foundation of the West as Confucious is the foundation of much of the east.
column7:
In response to the Sophists, Plato and Aristotle articulated the importance of Paideia, and proposed some ideas of what it might look like.

The deliberate cultivation and transmission of everything a civilization needs its citizens to understand, know, believe, and be capable of. Not just facts. Not just skills. The full formation of a person capable of participating in, sustaining, and improving a free and just society.

Plato tried to build it at the first Academy. The liberal arts were an attempt to institutionalize it, the skills "worthy of a free person," the capacities needed for self-governance. The entire tradition of what we now call the humanities was, at its root, an answer to the telephone problem: how do you transmit the signal without degrading it?

Terms are imperfect vessels for concepts. They are vulnerable to misunderstanding, mishearing, misinterpreting, and active corruption.

Every time an idea passes from one mind to another, some of the concept can be lost. The more complex the idea, the greater the risk of loss in transmission. This is how the Liberal Arts went from "the skills necessary to take active and effective part in civic life" to "a worthless degree."

Over enough transmissions, the understanding is gone. The people who can repeat the words can no longer understand their original meaning.

This erosion is not inevitable.

The pattern recurs because we have never built a sufficiently reliable mechanism for transmitting concepts. We've built mass literacy programs, mass media systems, but never a mass wisdom program.

Not once in all of history.
column9:
Werner Jaeger, Paideia: The Ideals of Greek Culture (1933–1945, 3 volumes) — the definitive modern treatment. Argues Paideia was the Greeks' central contribution to civilization, not democracy or philosophy in the narrow sense, but the concept of deliberate human formation.

Latin equivalent: humanitas (Cicero, Aulus Gellius). German: Bildung — cultural self-formation through education and experience. Each carries similar connotations; each has degraded in its own language.

"Credentials without substance" — current stats: average US student debt ~$37,000 (2024). Tuition has risen ~1,200% since 1980, adjusted for inflation. Meanwhile employer surveys consistently show graduates lack critical thinking and communication skills — the very things the liberal arts were supposed to provide.

See existing Notes column for Academy etymology and Paideia/Confucius comparison.

Column 6 contains an extended draft connecting Paideia to Plato's Academy and the liberal arts tradition. Material was incorporated into the final Rework but the longer version has useful detail about Aristotle's specific curriculum.

column4:
Terms are imperfect vessels for concepts. They are vulnerable to misunderstanding, mishearing, misinterpreting, and active corruption.

Every time an idea passes from one mind to another, some of the concept can be lost. The more complex the idea, the greater the risk of loss in transmission. This is how the Liberal Arts went from being perceived as "the skills necessary to take active and effective part in civic life" to "a worthless degree."

Over enough transmissions, and without painstaking precision, the understanding disappears. The people who can repeat the words can no longer understand their original meaning.

This erosion is not inevitable.

The pattern recurs because we have never built a sufficiently reliable mechanism for transmitting concepts. We've built mass literacy programs, mass media systems, post-scarcity information, but never a mass wisdom program.

Not once in all of history, but now we have built many of the building blocks to achieve it.
column6:
Dawkins' concept of the "meme" was, is another, a recognition of this same problem: ideas replicate imperfectly, just as genes do. The concept was not novel — Paideia, memetic corruption, lossy transmission are all descriptions of the same phenomenon across different eras. 

The irony that "meme" itself mutated almost immediately from a serious concept into an internet joke is  both unfortunate and almost too perfect an illustration of the mechanism it describes.
column9:
Linguistic/philosophical background for "terms as vessels":
- Saussure's distinction between signifier (the word) and signified (the concept). The relationship is arbitrary and conventional — which is why it can drift.
- Wittgenstein's language games — meaning is use, and use changes across communities and time.
- Korzybski: "the map is not the territory" (Science and Sanity, 1933). Words are maps of concepts, not the concepts themselves.
- The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis (weak form): language shapes thought. If the terms degrade, the thinking degrades with them.

"Liberal Arts to worthless degree" — the original seven: grammar, logic, rhetoric (trivium) + arithmetic, geometry, music, astronomy (quadrivium). Codified by Martianus Capella (5th century) and Cassiodorus (6th century). These were the prerequisites for all further study — you learned these first because without them you couldn't learn anything else properly. Now they're electives at best.

"Never built a mass wisdom program" — mass literacy is recent (global literacy rate was ~12% in 1820, ~86% in 2015). Mass media is ~100 years old. Post-scarcity information is ~25 years old. The building blocks for mass wisdom transmission exist for the first time in history. We have the infrastructure; we lack the content and the institution.

See existing Notes column for Dawkins meme irony.

column4: Fundamental questions If you ask why enough times, all of this traces back to the same place. There are questions we may never answer: • Why is there something rather than nothing, and what can we know about reality? • What is the right way to live? • How do we come to know anything anyway? These are not rhetorical, every serious question comes from trying to answer them. They may never be answered completely. Some may remain permanently open. But that does not make them irrelevant. It makes them fundamental.
column6: Much of what follows is not new. These ideas have been articulated, in various forms, by people far more qualified than me — across thousands of years, in dozens of traditions. What may be new is the synthesis, the accessibility, and the attempt to build it as a living, connected system rather than isolated academic papers. And much of it might simply be new to you. That is enough to begin.
column9:
The three questions and their philosophical lineages:
- "Why is there something rather than nothing?" — Leibniz, Principles of Nature and Grace (1714). Though versions appear in pre-Socratic philosophy (Parmenides: "what is, is; what is not, is not").
- "What is the right way to live?" — Socrates in the Republic: "We are discussing no small matter, but how we ought to live." Also the central question of Confucius's Analects, the Bhagavad Gita, and arguably every religious text.
- "How do we come to know?" — the domain of epistemology. Plato's Theaetetus. Descartes' Meditations (1641). Hume's problem of induction. Kant's three Critiques map loosely onto these three questions.

"Every serious question comes from trying to answer them" — example chain: Why is healthcare expensive? → What do we owe each other? → How should we structure society? → What is the right way to live? → What is goodness? → Why are we here? Every "why" chain terminates at a fundamental question.

See existing Notes column for the humility disclaimer ("Much of what follows is not new...").

column4: The Value of Ignorance A civilization that abandons such questions does not become practical. It becomes shallow. It loses the ability to orient itself, it loses its frame, its starting point, ignorance. Accepting our ignorance is foundational. The awareness that we don't know, and the refusal to pretend otherwise, is what separates the pursuit of wisdom from the performance of it. An honest answer of "I don't know" should be met with respect for the opportunity it contains, not shame for the ignorance it admits. Ignorance is the root of wisdom. It is the necessary starting point for inquiry. It is the beginning of seriousness. Accurate confidence in our own beliefs is hard and we have to fight our own minds to get there. "The ancient Oracle said that I was the wisest of all the Greeks. It is because I alone, of all the Greeks, know that I know nothing." — Socrates, Apology "What is the first business of him who philosophizes? To throw away self-conceit. For it is impossible for a man to begin to learn that which he thinks that he knows." — Epictetus, Discourses
column9:
Socrates quote: Plato, Apology 21d. The Oracle at Delphi (Pythia) declared no one wiser. His investigation — testing the claim against politicians, poets, and craftsmen — is the narrative arc of the Apology. Each group believed they knew; Socrates found they didn't but also didn't know they didn't. His only advantage was knowing.

Epictetus quote: Discourses, Book II, Chapter 17. Epictetus (c. 50–135 CE) was born a slave, studied under Musonius Rufus, freed, taught in Nicopolis. His Discourses were transcribed by his student Arrian — themselves a transmission across generations, surviving only because someone wrote them down. The full text is available on this site.

"Fight our own minds to get there" — relevant cognitive science: confirmation bias, motivated reasoning, the Dunning-Kruger effect (Kruger & Dunning, 1999 — people with low competence systematically overestimate their ability; Socrates identified the pattern 2,400 years earlier), the backfire effect (Nyhan & Reifler, 2010 — correcting a false belief can paradoxically strengthen it). Epistemic humility is not natural; it is trained. Which is why the Academy matters.

"A civilization that abandons such questions does not become practical. It becomes shallow." — Compare with Chesterton: "The madman is not the man who has lost his reason. The madman is the man who has lost everything except his reason." (Orthodoxy, 1908). A civilization that keeps its technical capacity but loses its fundamental orientation is not sane — it is efficient at nothing in particular.

column4: Wisdom Zhi, Sophia, Geist Maat, Saraswati, Athena Socrates, Buddha, Confucius Wisdom is conceptualized, personified, and sought cross-culturally. Different names, different traditions, each trying to narrow in on the same thing. We named ourselves Homo sapiens, the wise ones, in recognition of our, so far, unique capacity to gain it. Philosophy is a term that describes the relationship we should have with Wisdom. Philia is not simply love, as it often gets translated in English. It is the most noble relationship you can have with anything, a deep abiding reciprocal bond. The commitment to sit with hard questions, tolerate the irritation of precision, maintain the rigor of logic even when stories are cheaper and feelings easier. Few things pursued give back to the pursuer as much as pursuing wisdom. Wisdom is not reason, though it requires an understanding of how to get at truth. Wisdom is not knowledge, though it requires facts about reality. Wisdom is not moral discernment, though it requires an understanding of the right way to live. Wisdom is the integration of these three capacities. Taken together, they allow a person, or a civilization, to navigate complex unknowns and move forward. It is the animating force, the spirit, that drives us to understand, to explore, to discover what lies in the light.
column9:
The cross-cultural list unpacked:

Concepts:
- Zhi (知/智): Confucian. One of the five constant virtues (alongside Ren, Li, Yi, Xin). "To know what you know and what you do not know — that is true wisdom" (Analects 2.17). Deep practical wisdom — not abstract knowledge but insight that enables ethical action.
- Sophia (Σοφία): Greek. Root of "philosophy." Personified in Proverbs 8:22–31 as present at creation. Hagia Sophia (537 CE) = "Holy Wisdom." The building itself is a civilizational statement: the highest aspiration is wisdom.
- Geist: German. Hegel, Phenomenology of Spirit (1807). Not easily translated — spirit, mind, collective consciousness of an era. "The Geist fades" describes the erosion in one phrase.

Personifications:
- Maat (𓁦): Egyptian. Goddess/principle of truth, justice, cosmic order. The 42 negative confessions. The weighing of the heart. Foundational to Egyptian civilization for ~3,000 years.
- Saraswati (सरस्वती): Hindu goddess of knowledge, music, arts, wisdom. Depicted with a veena, a book, and a rosary — art, knowledge, and devotion unified in one figure.
- Athena (Ἀθηνᾶ): Greek goddess of wisdom and strategic warfare (not chaotic violence — that's Ares). Born fully formed from Zeus's head. Athens named itself after her — the city is literally named for wisdom.

People:
- Socrates (470–399 BCE), Buddha (Siddhartha Gautama, c. 563–483 BCE), Confucius (Kong Qiu, 551–479 BCE). Three independent civilizations produced foundational wisdom-seekers within roughly the same century. Karl Jaspers called this the Axial Age (The Origin and Goal of History, 1949) — the period when humanity first became conscious of itself as a whole.

Philia: Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics, Books VIII–IX. Distinguishes friendships of utility, pleasure, and virtue. Philosophy = philia + sophia = the sustained commitment to wisdom. See also Row 17 notes on the corruption of philia as a suffix.

"Wisdom is the integration of these three capacities" — this formulation is the core claim of the entire project. Traditions that held all three together (Rta, arguably Tao in its pre-Confucian/pre-Laozi form) maintained the integration. Traditions that specialized in one branch (Laozi's Dao → Reality, Confucian Dao → Right, Stoic Logos → Reason) achieved depth at the cost of wholeness. The erosion pattern at the civilizational level mirrors the specialization pattern at the philosophical level.

column4: Reason The spirit of wisdom drives us to understand. Reason is how we come to know. Reason is the development and refinement of methods for distinguishing belief from knowledge. Logic, epistemology, hypothesis, experiment, falsifiability, discourse. These are some of the tools of Reason. Tools we have built, sharpened, and refined over millennia to discipline our thinking against the biases and shortcuts that make us confident in things we should doubt. But Reason is not a fixed toolkit. The methods themselves must be examined, stress-tested, and updated. This is what the philosophy of science does, and why it matters, it is how we earn confidence in what we believe.
column5: A word bad psychologist of the last 100 years have used to associate with disease. Understanding lost in transmission.
column9:
Tools of Reason with origins:
- Logic: Aristotle's Organon (4th century BCE). Syllogistic reasoning. Later: Stoic logic, medieval logic, Boolean algebra (Boole, 1847), predicate logic (Frege, 1879), modal logic (Kripke, 1959).
- Epistemology: "How do we know what we know?" Plato's Theaetetus. Descartes' Meditations (1641). Hume's problem of induction. Gettier problems (1963) — showing justified true belief isn't quite sufficient.
- Hypothesis and experiment: Francis Bacon, Novum Organum (1620). The inductive method. The empirical turn that launched modern science.
- Falsifiability: Karl Popper, The Logic of Scientific Discovery (1934/1959). A claim is scientific only if it could in principle be shown false. This was itself an update to the toolkit — the method improving itself.
- Discourse: See the 3D's of Discourse article on this site (Deliberation, Dialogue, Debate). Civil discourse is itself a tool of Reason — the means by which reasoning happens between people rather than just within one mind.

"The methods themselves must be examined" — Thomas Kuhn, The Structure of Scientific Revolutions (1962). Paradigm shifts. Also Lakatos (research programmes), Feyerabend (Against Method — arguing no single method governs all scientific progress), Bayesian epistemology.

Aquinas's distinction (Summa Theologica): discursive reasoning (ratio) = step-by-step, human; intuitive understanding (intellectus) = immediate, divine. The human mode is sequential and effortful. This is not a deficiency — it is the distinctly human way of knowing. But its cost is why it erodes.

Column 4 note about "a word bad psychologists corrupted" — this refers to the suffix -philia being pathologized. Connects to the philia discussion in Row 12 (Wisdom) and the fuller aside in Row 17's existing notes.

See also Row 22 existing Notes column: "Reason's methods extend far beyond the hard science laboratory..." — this material about social science needing philosophy was considered for the Reason section but works better as a supporting note.

column4: Reality What is the nature of reality and how does it work? This has been an ongoing effort for millennia. Starting with Aristotle's Natural Philosophy, what we might now call 'the Sciences'. The long slow work of mapping the territory. We've come far. From believing the spirits of the seas, sun, and moon were Poseidon, Apollo, and Artemis, to gaining knowledge of gravity, the atom, the genome. Understanding gravity clarified our place in the cosmos while forcing us to wrestle with how lucky and small we are. Understanding the atom gave us energy to be used to destroy and create. Understanding the genome gives us new medicine and brings up difficult moral questions. Every advance in understanding the territory complicates the question of what we ought to do with that knowledge.
column9:
"Starting with Aristotle's Natural Philosophy" — Physics, De Caelo, De Generatione et Corruptione, Meteorologica. Aristotle established systematic empirical observation even though he got many specifics wrong (geocentrism, spontaneous generation). The practice mattered more than the conclusions.

The mythological-to-mechanistic progression:
- Thales of Miletus (c. 624–546 BCE): first to seek natural rather than supernatural explanations. "The primary substance is water." Wrong, but asking the right kind of question.
- Poseidon → tides → lunar gravity (Newton, Principia, 1687)
- Apollo → the sun → nuclear fusion (Bethe, 1939)
- "Spirits" of disease → germ theory (Pasteur, Koch, 1860s–1880s)
Each case: the observation was real, the personification was imprecise, the mechanism was discoverable. The myth was clever, just data-poor.

"Every advance complicates what we ought to do":
- Gravity → cosmology → we are cosmically insignificant. Existential reckoning.
- The atom → nuclear energy → Hiroshima and clean energy. Same knowledge, opposite applications.
- The genome → CRISPR (Doudna & Charpentier, 2012) → gene therapy AND designer babies AND eugenics concerns.
- AI (current) → the most powerful information technology ever built, trained on the collective output of human civilization. Who controls it? Toward what ends? With what accountability? These are Right questions forced into urgency by Reality advances.

Rta (ऋत), the Vedic concept of cosmic order, is arguably the oldest attempt at what this section describes — the claim that there IS an underlying order to reality, prior to the gods themselves. Our exploration of Reality is the attempt to map what Rta asserts exists.

column4: Right How do we define our goals, reassess them, and make sure we are oriented toward them? Reality tells us what is. Reason tells us how to think about it. Right is what we decide to do about it. Many traditions have built laws to codify this: Nomos, Li, Shari'a, Halakha. And many traditions have a separate concept for the principle by which those laws get judged, corrected, or revised: Dikaiosynē, Yi, Adl, Tzedek. Right is both. We learn more about reality, improve our reasoning, and face changing conditions, so we must continually reassess our ends, the means by which we pursue them, and the way we orient ourselves toward both. We are burdened with imperfect knowledge, scarce resources, competing interests, and consequences that fall on real people. How sure are we that we are working toward something good, and how well is society aimed toward achieving it? What is goodness? What is justice? How do we balance the competing demands of current well-being and future well-being, and what do we do when we disagree on what is Right? How do we balance resources between moving our own society closer to the Right way to live, protecting it from threats, and aligning other societies to what we believe to be Right? Right is not merely ethics in the academic sense. It is the ongoing assessment of whether a society is on track, and to where.
column9:
The two-layer distinction:

Laws (codified Right at a point in time):
- Nomos (νόμος): Greek. Law, custom, convention. The nomos/physis debate (convention vs nature) was central to Sophistic and Socratic philosophy — are laws natural or made up?
- Li (禮): Confucian ritual propriety. Governs the five relationships (ruler-subject, parent-child, husband-wife, elder-younger, friend-friend). Analects Book XII.
- Shari'a (شريعة): "the path to water." Islamic law from Quran, Hadith, ijma (consensus), qiyas (analogy).
- Halakha (הלכה): "the way to walk." Jewish law from Torah and Talmud.

Principles by which laws are judged and updated:
- Dikaiosyne (δικαιοσύνη): Greek justice. Aristotle NE Book V: "complete virtue in relation to our fellow humans." The Republic is structured entirely around defining it.
- Yi (義): Confucian righteousness. The moral judgment that sits above Li. One of the five constant virtues.
- Adl (عدل): Islamic justice/equity. One of the 99 names of God (Al-Adl). The principle by which Shari'a is evaluated.
- Tzedek (צדק): Hebrew justice. "Justice, justice shall you pursue" (Deuteronomy 16:20 — repetition deliberate). Root of tzedakah.

Every tradition built both — the code AND the concept that sits above the code and judges whether the code is still right. The code is the snapshot; the principle is the living capacity to revise it.

Dharma (धर्म) straddles both layers — it is simultaneously the prescribed duty AND the principle of rightness by which that duty is assessed. This may be why it resists clean categorization.

"Right is not merely ethics in the academic sense" — academic ethics has largely become a spectator sport: analyzing trolley problems rather than asking whether a civilization is on track. Applied ethics (bioethics, AI ethics) is closer but still tends to be reactive. Right as used here is closer to what the Greeks meant by politike — the art of steering the polis.

column4: Wisdom, again Wisdom is an animating force, but a fragile one. It requires stability, surplus, freedom of inquiry, time, precision, and institutions that reward truth-seeking over credential-seeking. These conditions do not maintain themselves. Reason gives us tools to discover Reality. Reality gives us tools to work toward the Right way to live. Right helps direct resources - time, talent, technology - back into the refinement of Reason. It is a compounding cycle, and the full relationship between the three is richer than is captured in this summary. Yet because wisdom is tied to reality and truth rather than mere convention, it can be recovered. Foundations can be rebuilt.
column9:
The compounding cycle:
- Francis Bacon, Novum Organum (1620): "Knowledge is power" — the first explicit statement that understanding reality gives practical capacity.
- Condorcet, Sketch for a Historical Picture of the Progress of the Human Mind (1795): reason + science + rights = compounding human welfare. Written while hiding from the Terror. He believed in the cycle even as the Revolution devoured its own.
- D.M. Berwick: "Not all change is improvement, but all improvement is change." The progressive-conservative tension in one sentence.

"Wisdom can be recovered" — the Renaissance as proof of concept: Greek and Roman texts preserved by Islamic scholars (House of Wisdom, Baghdad, 8th–13th centuries; translation schools of Córdoba and Toledo) were reintroduced to Europe in the 12th–15th centuries. Wisdom tied to truth survives even centuries of neglect — if the texts survive. This is one reason the web app exists: digital text doesn't burn.

"The full relationship between the three is richer than captured in this summary" — intentional understatement. Reality informs Right (what's possible constrains what's desirable). Right directs Reason (values determine what questions we prioritize). Reason checks Right (logic tests whether our values are internally consistent). Each pair has a bidirectional relationship, and all three interact simultaneously. The articles on Power and Paideia develop these dynamics further.

column4: Foundational Understanding This is my attempt to reorient our aim. It begins where all foundations must begin: with questions we cannot yet answer, that we may never answer, and the tools we have for approaching them anyway. We need to clarify where we came from. Recover the purpose of Paideia. Rebuild the Academy. Remove the rot in the Temples, and a bit more. Understand the civilizational machinery required for wisdom to persist. What follows is a series of ideas on where we should be going, and how to think about going there. To help civilization remain sane, free, self-correcting, and capable of transmitting the substance of understanding. That is the problem. That is the inheritance. That is the work.
column6: Aside on philia: The word has not merely been diluted but actively corrupted. Its most common modern appearance is as a suffix in clinical psychology — paraphilias, meaning disordered sexual interests — giving philia a pathological connotation it never carried. Aristotle's philia was chosen, reciprocal, disciplined commitment. A philosopher isn't someone who lusts for wisdom — that would be an ero-sopher. They're someone who has a sustained, caring relationship with it, like a craftsman's relationship to their craft.
column9:
See existing Notes column for the philia aside (paraphilia corruption). Consider whether this note belongs here or in Row 12 (Wisdom), since philia is introduced in the article at the Wisdom section.

"Remove the rot in the Temples, and a bit more" — the "bit more" encompasses: political spectrums (how we talk about aiming), power (what prevents good ideas from winning), and economic questions (post-scarcity, AI, displacement costs). These are addressed in the follow-up articles.

"That is the problem. That is the inheritance. That is the work." — three beats that function as a commitment. The problem (erosion). The inheritance (the tradition of people who fought it before). The work (what we do now).

column4:
Up Next:
Erosion - Where are we now and what causes this cycle to fail?
Power - 4 domains of power, how can we understand power to maintain this cycle?
Paideia - 6 institutions of education, a deeper dive into the history of Paideia and a model for educational institutions we need to make it work.
column5: The Spirit of Discovery From this acceptance grows the motive: discovery. If we don't know, and we're aware that we don't know, and we believe we might be capable of knowing more than we currently do we've found a Foundation. Then the question becomes: how do we come to know more?
column8: Reason The spirit of wisdom drives us to understand. Reason is how. Reason is the development and refinement of methods for distinguishing what is true from what merely feels true. Logic, epistemology, hypothesis, experiment, falisfability, discourse are tools of Reason. Tools we have built, sharpened, and refined, over millennia, to discipline our thinking against the biases and shortcuts that make us confident in things we should doubt. Improving our toolbox is what the philosophy of science does, and why it matters. How we turn beliefs into knowledge. Reason extends far beyond the laboratory. In domains where clean experiments are impossible, we still need clear thinking to improve understanding: social science, governance, ethics. Good social science requires good philosophy precisely because the experiments are hard to run. You can't isolate variables when your subjects are adaptive or reactive, your timescales are generational, and population measured in millions or billions and sample size is hundreds. This is where the art of reason, logic, careful inference, epistemic humility, becomes hardest. Acquiring this knowledge hasn't been easy, partly because society erodes, and finding the Right Way to produce a Good Life or Just City hasn't been easy either. Accurate confidence in our beliefs is a core tenant of good reason. Know Thyself.
column9:
The three follow-ups address the three major gaps this article leaves open:
- Erosion: The mechanism. WHY does the cycle fail? Pathos/Mythos/Ethos/Logos as modes of engaging with truth, each with its own decay pattern. The demand side (Sneaky Candy). The supply side (institutional failure).
- Power: The obstacle. Why can't good ideas just win? Because ideas need power to become reality, and power corrupts the process. The 4 domains (Physical, Resources, Social, Ideational) and the cascade when the ideational domain weakens.
- Paideia: The solution. What institutions do we need? The 6 educational functions every civilization requires (Academy, Guilds, Laboratory, News, Agora, Temple), how all are currently failing, and what rebuilding looks like. The Grandfather Principle.

These three plus this Foundations article form the core chain. Everything else — political spectrums, specific policy questions, breakdowns of current events — branches from them.

column3: Acquiring this knowledge hasn't been easy, its taken us thousands of years to get here. Partly because society erodes, and finding the Right Way to produce a Good Life or Just City hasn't been easy either.
column5: Each of the underlined is a reference to a work of philosophy trying to find the Right way to life. Ethics, Republic, Analects
column8:
Reason is humanity's capacity to find truth. Our, so far, unique ability to discipline our minds toward a process of connecting observations to conclusions with logic, evidence, and scrutiny. It is effortful. It is sequential. Aquinas distinguished it from divine understanding: God grasps truth whole; humans must chase it step by laborious step. The mind running between ideas to connect them is not a deficiency. It is the distinctly human way of knowing.

We developed methods to formalize this capacity. The scientific method — itself a living, evolving set of tools — is reason's sharpest edge. Empiricism and old Rationalism working together. Falsifiability, replication, peer review. These tools are not perfect and they are not finished. A hundred years ago, Popper's contribution wasn't even established. We will keep refining.

But reason extends beyond the laboratory. In domains where clean experiments are impossible — social science, governance, ethics — we still need to think clearly. Good social science requires good philosophy precisely because the experiments are hard to run. You can't isolate variables when your subjects are adaptive or reactive, your timescales are generational, and your sample size is one civilization. This is where the art of reason — logic, careful inference, epistemic humility — matters most.

column3:
We now have a foundational purpose, even if not yet a fundamental one:
• We can Reason
. • Which allows us to learn about Reality
. • As such have a temporary answer to Why are we here?[3].
column5: We've made great progress, we've gone from believing the spirit of the seas, sun, and moon were Poseidon, Apollo, and Artemis to gaining knowledge of gravity. We've split the atom, and cracked the genome.

column3: Find the Right way to live so that we can pass down our Reason and understanding of Reality to get closer to answering Fundamental Questions.

Or in short, seek Wisdom, the Foundational Value of 'the West'. It is what the Renaissance was trying to bring back and what strived the Enlightenment strived to realize. Yet the spirit of Athena, of Socrates, the Geist fades.
column5:
Reason
The spirit of wisdom drives us to understand. Reason is how we come to know. 

Reason is the development and refinement of methods for distinguishing belief from knowledge. Logic, epistemology, hypothesis, experiment, falsifiability, discourse. These are some of the tools of Reason. Tools we have built, sharpened, and refined over millennia to discipline our thinking against the biases and shortcuts that make us confident in things we should doubt.

But Reason is not a fixed toolkit. The methods themselves must be examined, stress-tested, and updated. This is what the philosophy of science does, and why it matters, it is how we accurately increase the confidence in our own beliefs. 

And we've applied it to both understanding the cosmos, Reality, and working toward the Right way to live. 

Reality
What is the nature of reality and how does it work? 

Natural Philosophy, the hard sciences, the long slow work of mapping the territory.

We've made great progress, we've gone from believing the spirit of the seas, sun, and moon were Poseidon, Apollo, and Artemis to gaining knowledge of gravity. Understanding the atom gave us energy to be used to destroy and create. Understanding the genome gives us medicine and brings up difficult moral questions. Understanding the climate gives us forecasts and obligations to the future generations. 

Advancements in understanding the territory complicates the question of what we ought to do with that knowledge. 

Right
Right is defining a goal, re-assessing it, and making sure we oriented toward it. How sure are we that we are working toward something good, and how good is our aim for getting there? 

Is the society functioning in a way that serves our interests? How do we balance competing interests of current well-being, future well-being, and what do we do when we disagree on what we think well-being is? How much do we spend on protecting ourselves from the other group that thinks we are doing harm, vs advancements in healthcare, education, and investing in abundance?

Right is not merely ethics in the academic sense. It is the ongoing assessment of wether society is on track. Wisdom is not the only animating force, and it is one of the most fragile. It requires,  some stability, surplus, freedom of inquiry, institutions that reward truth-seeking over credential-seeking. These conditions do not maintain themselves.
column6: Reasons methods extend far beyond the hard science laboratory. In domains where clean experiments are impossible, we still need clear thinking to improve understanding: social science, governance, economics, ethics. Good social science requires good philosophy precisely because the experiments are hard to run. You cannot isolate variables when your subjects are adaptive and reactive, your timescales are generational, and your populations are measured in billions while your sample sizes are measured in hundreds. This is where reason is truly tested, is hardest, and matters most.
column7:
Reason extends far beyond the laboratory. In domains where clean experiments are impossible, we still need clear thinking to improve understanding: social science, governance, ethics. Good social science requires good philosophy precisely because the experiments are hard to run. You cannot isolate variables when your subjects are adaptive and reactive, your timescales are generational, and your populations are measured in billions while your sample sizes are measured in hundreds. This is where the discipline of reason — logic, careful inference, epistemic humility — becomes hardest and matters most.

Accurate confidence in our beliefs is a core tenet of good reason. Knowing what you know, knowing what you don't, and calibrating your certainty to what the evidence actually supports. The Socratic "Know Thyself" is not a vague spiritual suggestion — it is a demand for epistemic honesty. The beginning of reason, like the beginning of philosophy, is the admission that you might be wrong.

Reason gives us methods. But methods are only as good as what they are tested against. The territory they must account for is not abstract — it is the cosmos as it actually is.

column3:
Yet...Wisdom is elusive, working toward the Right way to live, learning about Reality, and improving our ability to Reason is fraught with a variety of problems.
• Its expensive.
• It takes a long time.
• As society erodes we risk diverging from our Foundational Purpose.
• It requires sufficient power to overcome different ideologies while also improving our own.
